title: The Method of Centering Prayer
text: Centering Prayer is a method of reducing the obstacles to the gift of contemplative prayer and of facilitating the development of the contemplative dimension of the Gospel. The method is simple: choose a sacred word as the symbol of your intention to consent to God's presence and action within you. When thoughts arise, return ever-so-gently to the sacred word.
reference: Thomas Keating, Open Mind, Open Heart

title: Consenting to God
text: The sacred word is not a mantra. It is not repeated constantly. It is only used when one realizes one has engaged with a thought. It is the symbol — the consent — not the act of prayer itself. The prayer itself is the intention: to be present to God and to consent to his presence and action within. When you return to the sacred word, you are not starting over. You are expressing the same consent you made at the beginning.
reference: Thomas Keating, Open Mind, Open Heart

title: The Divine Therapist
text: God is like a perfect therapist who, when given our consent, begins to heal us from the inside out. The unconscious is a storehouse of the emotional wounds of a lifetime — the unmet needs of childhood, the buried grief, the defended wounds. In deep contemplative prayer, God gently brings these to the surface, not to punish us, but to heal us. The unloading of the unconscious is not a distraction in prayer; it is the prayer working.

title: The Fruit of Centering Prayer
text: The fruit of Centering Prayer is not a feeling of peace or a spiritual experience during the time of prayer. The fruit is the transformation of one's life. The gentleness, the patience, the freedom from compulsion, the capacity for love — these are the signs that contemplative prayer is doing its work. Do not judge the prayer by what you experience in it, but by what you become.
reference: Thomas Keating, Open Mind, Open Heart

title: Beyond Thinking
text: In Centering Prayer, we move beyond thinking. Not because thinking is bad — it is wonderful. But because God is beyond what thinking can reach. The deepest communication with God is not through thoughts about God, however beautiful they may be, but through a simple resting in God's presence — a consent, a surrender, a turning of the whole self toward the Ground of Being in which we live and move.

title: The Welcoming Prayer
text: The Welcoming Prayer is the practice of welcoming into consciousness what is actually present — feeling, emotion, sensation — without trying to change, fix, or escape it. When we welcome what is difficult, we are practicing a radical trust in God's love. We are saying: this too — even this — is held by God. And in this welcoming, the difficult thing often softens and dissolves, not because we forced it to, but because we released it to God.
reference: Thomas Keating, The Heart of the World

title: The Three Programs of Happiness
text: Every human being has, from early life, developed three programs for happiness: seeking security, seeking affirmation, and seeking power or control. These are not evil in themselves. But when they become the organizing principle of our lives — when we demand that they be satisfied — they become the source of all our suffering. Contemplative practice gradually loosens the grip of these programs, not by fighting them, but by offering the deeper satisfaction that only God can give.

title: God's First Language
text: God's first language is silence. Everything else is a translation. In order to hear God speak, we must become comfortable with silence — interior silence, not just exterior quiet. This interior silence is the soil in which the seed of divine love grows. Without it, even the most beautiful words about God remain on the surface of the soul. In silence, they take root.
reference: Thomas Keating, Open Mind, Open Heart

title: Lectio Divina
text: Lectio Divina — divine reading — is the ancient Christian practice of reading Scripture slowly, as a means of encounter with God. One reads not to gather information but to receive a word, a phrase, an image that the Spirit brings alive. One lingers with it, turns it over in the heart, prays from it, and finally rests in the silence to which it leads. Lectio, meditatio, oratio, contemplatio — reading, reflecting, responding, resting.

title: The False Self
text: The false self is the self that is organized around the need for esteem, affection, survival, and power. It is the self we present to the world. It is not evil; it is simply incomplete. The true self is the self that is grounded in God — the self as God knows it, loves it, and sustains it at every moment. The journey of the spiritual life is the journey from the false self to the true self — from the self we have made to the self God has always been making.

title: The Night of Sense
text: The night of sense is when God withdraws the consolations of prayer — the felt sweetness, the sense of his presence, the ease of devotion. This is not a punishment. It is an invitation to a more mature faith — a faith that is grounded not in feeling but in truth. The soul is being weaned from spiritual self-seeking and led into a purer love. The dryness itself is the prayer, if the soul perseveres with patience and trust.

title: Presence and Receptivity
text: Contemplation is not something we do. It is something we receive. Our task is presence and receptivity — showing up, remaining open, not filling the silence with our own agenda. The contemplative posture is not effort but surrender. Not grasping but releasing. The deepest prayer is not 'Lord, give me' but 'Lord, I am here.' And the deepest answer to that prayer is not what God gives, but what God is — Love, giving itself endlessly.
reference: Thomas Keating, Open Mind, Open Heart

title: The Community of Prayer
text: No one travels the contemplative path alone. The desert fathers had their communities. The medieval monastics had their communities. Centering Prayer is practiced best in community — groups that meet regularly, pray together, support one another through the dry times, and celebrate together the slow transformation that prayer brings. We need companions for this journey. The solitude is real, but it is shared.
reference: Thomas Keating, Foundations for Centering Prayer

title: The Interior Journey
text: The spiritual journey is the development of trust in God at ever deeper levels. We begin by trusting God with small things. As we grow, we trust him with larger things — our needs, our fears, our relationships. And in the deepest contemplation, we trust him with ourselves — not the self we have curated, but the self we are afraid of, the self we have hidden. This final surrender is the beginning of true freedom.

title: Transformation, Not Information
text: The goal of the spiritual life is not information about God but transformation into God — the gradual conforming of the human person to the image of Christ. This does not happen through study alone, or through good works alone, or through any single practice. It happens through the long, patient, faithful turning of the whole self toward God — in prayer, in service, in suffering, in joy — until one can say with Paul: 'It is no longer I who live, but Christ who lives in me.'
